bytes.topic: WebSockets — connection lifecycle, heartbeat, reconnection, rooms
bytes.title: WebSockets — Connection Lifecycle, Heartbeat, Reconnection, and Rooms
bytes.body:
A WebSocket connection transitions through four readyStates: CONNECTING (0), OPEN (1), CLOSING (2), CLOSED (3). Most bugs come from writing to a socket before it's OPEN or after the server has already closed it — always guard sends with `if (ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN)`.

Heartbeats keep NAT gateways and load balancers from silently dropping idle connections. The standard pattern: client sends a `ping` message every 30s, server echoes a `pong`. If no pong arrives within a timeout window, treat the connection as dead and trigger reconnect.

Reconnection should use exponential backoff with jitter — reconnecting 1000 clients simultaneously after a server restart creates a thundering herd. Cap backoff at ~30s and add `Math.random() * 1000` ms of jitter.

Rooms are a server-side abstraction: a `Map<string, Set<WebSocket>>` keyed by room ID. On message, iterate the room's set and call `client.send()` only if `client.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN`. Crucially, remove clients from all rooms inside the `ws.on('close', ...)` handler to prevent memory leaks and phantom broadcasts.

bytes.topic: tRPC — end-to-end type safety, procedure types, React Query integration
bytes.title: WebSockets + tRPC — Type-Safe Subscriptions with React Query
bytes.body: tRPC's `subscription` procedure type maps directly to a persistent WebSocket connection via `@trpc/server/adapters/ws`. On the server, a subscription returns an `observable<T>`, and the WS adapter keeps that stream open over a single WebSocket frame channel — no SSE fallback, no polling. The client uses `createWSClient` paired with `wsLink`, typically composed with `splitLink` to route queries/mutations over HTTP and subscriptions over the WebSocket. This split is critical: HTTP procedures get batching and caching; subscriptions get the low-latency bidirectional channel they need. On the React side, `trpc.[procedure].useSubscription()` integrates directly with React Query's cache — incoming WebSocket messages can call `utils.[procedure].setData()` to invalidate or patch cached query state. The full type chain — from `observable<OutputType>` on the server to the inferred hook return on the client — is preserved end-to-end without codegen. Reconnect logic, including exponential backoff, is handled by `createWSClient`'s `retryDelay` option.

bytes.topic: gRPC — protobuf schemas, streaming types, when to use over REST
bytes.title: WebSockets vs gRPC Streaming — When to Drop the Raw Duplex Channel
bytes.body:
WebSocket connections give you a raw duplex channel — you own message framing, schema versioning, and multiplexing. gRPC bidirectional streaming (`stream Req returns (stream Res)`) gives you the same persistent-connection semantics over HTTP/2 but with protobuf contracts enforced at compile time, plus built-in flow control and connection multiplexing (multiple logical streams over one TCP connection).

gRPC defines four call types: unary, server streaming, client streaming, and bidirectional. Map these to your WebSocket patterns before migrating. The common WS pattern — client sends one message, server pushes N updates — maps directly to server streaming (`returns (stream T)`). True back-and-forth maps to bidirectional streaming, the closest analogue to a raw WebSocket.

Where WebSockets still win: browser-initiated bidirectional streams. gRPC-Web doesn't support client or bidirectional streaming in the browser — you'll still need WebSockets or SSE there. But in your service-to-service layer, gRPC replaces WebSockets cleanly: protobuf schemas eliminate the ad-hoc JSON envelopes you're likely maintaining, and HTTP/2 multiplexing cuts per-stream connection overhead significantly.

bytes.topic: REST — resource naming, HTTP verbs, status codes, idempotency
bytes.title: WebSockets Protocol Design — REST Semantics in a Persistent Connection
bytes.body:
WebSocket connections are stateful and long-lived — there are no per-message HTTP verbs or status codes baked in. But REST semantics still belong in your protocol design. The connection URL is your only shot at resource naming: `wss://api.example.com/feeds/{userId}` — keep it noun-based and hierarchical, not action-driven (`/connectFeed` is a REST anti-pattern that's even worse here).

Embed an `action` field in every message envelope — `subscribe`, `unsubscribe`, `publish`, `ack`. These map loosely to POST, DELETE, PUT, and a WebSocket-specific acknowledgement that REST doesn't need. For status, use WebSocket close codes (1000–1015 spec-defined, 4000–4999 application-defined) at the connection level, and mirror HTTP status semantics inside message payloads: `{ status: 404, error: "channel.not_found" }`.

Idempotency matters most on reconnect. Every outbound message should carry a `messageId`. On reconnect, replay unacknowledged messages — but the server must deduplicate by `messageId` to prevent double-processing. A Redis SET with a short TTL is the standard guard. Without this structure, your WebSocket API degrades into an untyped event bus that's impossible to audit, replay, or version.

bytes.topic: OpenAPI — spec-first vs code-first, generating clients, documentation
bytes.title: WebSockets and OpenAPI — Why You Need AsyncAPI Instead (and How to Generate Clients)
bytes.body:
OpenAPI doesn't model WebSocket connections — it's built around request/response HTTP semantics. WebSockets are bidirectional, message-driven, and stateful, so the correct spec format is AsyncAPI 3.x. Think of it as OpenAPI's counterpart for event-driven protocols.

Spec-first: write your AsyncAPI YAML defining channels (`ws://api/feed`), message schemas (`BytePublished`, `CommentAdded`), and bindings (`ws` protocol). Then run `asyncapi generate` to scaffold typed server stubs and client SDKs. Your `onmessage` handlers get generated with discriminated-union message types — no hand-rolling switch statements.

Code-first: use libraries like `tsoa` (HTTP only) or manually annotate handlers, then emit the spec. For raw WebSocket servers in Node.js (`ws` library), there's no auto-spec generation — you own the YAML. This makes spec-first the stronger default: the spec is your contract, and `asyncapi validate` catches schema drift before `WebSocket.send()` ships malformed frames.

For documentation, `asyncapi/studio` renders interactive channel diagrams with full message payloads. Pair it with `@asyncapi/react-component` to embed living docs in your developer portal.

bytes.topic: GraphQL — schema design, resolvers, N+1 with DataLoader, subscriptions
bytes.title: WebSockets + GraphQL Subscriptions — Per-Event DataLoader Scoping and N+1 in Real-Time Resolvers
bytes.body:
GraphQL subscriptions run over persistent WebSocket connections using the `graphql-ws` protocol (the modern successor to `subscriptions-transport-ws`). Each subscription resolver returns an `AsyncIterator` — when PubSub emits, every subscribed client's field resolvers fire against that payload independently.

N+1 behaves differently here than in HTTP queries. In a standard query, DataLoader batches within a single request tick. In subscriptions, each emitted event is its own execution context. If you scope DataLoader to the connection — `context()` firing once on `connection_init` — the loader cache goes stale across events, silently serving outdated data. You must scope a fresh DataLoader instance per-execution, not per-connection.

`graphql-ws`'s `context` callback receives `(ctx, message, args)`: the third argument signals an active operation execution, making it the right place to instantiate loaders. This gives you per-event batching without cross-event cache poisoning.

On schema design: avoid subscriptions that return only an ID and force clients to re-fetch over HTTP. Resolve the full type server-side and push it over the wire — one WebSocket frame replaces N round-trips per subscriber.

bytes.topic: Rate Limiting — per endpoint, per user, sliding window vs token bucket
bytes.title: WebSockets Rate Limiting — Sliding Window vs Token Bucket Per Connection
bytes.body:
Unlike HTTP, WebSocket connections are long-lived — a single socket can flood your server with thousands of frames per second. Rate limiting must happen at the message frame level, not just connection establishment.

Two practical strategies: **sliding window** tracks message timestamps in a rolling buffer (e.g., max 100 messages per 10s). It's precise but memory-intensive per connection. **Token bucket** grants each socket a bucket of tokens that refill at a fixed rate — burst-friendly and O(1) per message check.

For per-user limiting across multiple sockets (same user, multiple tabs), store state in Redis using the user's sub claim from the upgrade handshake JWT. Key pattern: `rl:{userId}:{endpoint}` — where endpoint maps to your message `type` field, not an HTTP route.

On violation, don't close the socket with `ws.terminate()` — that's abrupt. Send a typed control frame (`{ type: 'rate_limit_exceeded', retryAfter: 3000 }`) then call `ws.close(1008, 'Policy Violation')` with the correct WebSocket close code. Clients can back off gracefully using the `retryAfter` value.

bytes.topic: API Versioning — URL vs header versioning, backward compatibility strategy
bytes.title: WebSockets API Versioning — Subprotocol Negotiation vs URL Versioning
bytes.body:
WebSocket versioning operates across two layers: the handshake and the message envelope. URL versioning (`wss://api.example.com/v2/events`) is blunt — the version is locked the moment the upgrade completes, and you're managing separate connection pools per version. The idiomatic alternative is subprotocol negotiation via `Sec-WebSocket-Protocol`: the client proposes an ordered list (`['app-v2', 'app-v1']`), the server selects the highest it supports, and echoes it in the `101 Switching Protocols` response. Read `socket.protocol` on the client to drive schema selection.

For backward compatibility, maintain a version-dispatch layer server-side. Route incoming frames to versioned handlers based on the negotiated subprotocol rather than branching inside shared logic. Stamp your JSON envelope with a `v` field (`{ "v": 2, "type": "tick", "data": {} }`) so a single server instance can handle mixed-version clients across concurrent long-lived connections.

The critical footgun: hard-closing connections when deprecating a version. Instead, push a `{ "type": "deprecation", "deadline": "2026-06-01", "migrate_to": "app-v3" }` frame over the old subprotocol, enforce a grace window, then remove the old subprotocol from your server's accepted list during the next deploy.

bytes.topic: Pagination — cursor vs offset, total counts, infinite scroll patterns
bytes.title: WebSockets Pagination — Why Offsets Break Real-Time Feeds and How to Fix It
bytes.body:
Offset-based pagination collapses in WebSocket-driven feeds. If your server pushes 10 new messages while a client is scrolling, `FETCH_PAGE offset=20` silently skips or duplicates items — there are no HTTP headers to signal this shift. Cursor pagination solves this: anchor each page request to a stable `last_id` or ISO timestamp from the final item the client received.

Since WebSockets have no built-in pagination semantics, all metadata travels inside your message payload. Avoid returning `total_count` — it's stale the moment the server emits it and forces an expensive `COUNT(*)`. Instead, return a `has_more: boolean` derived from fetching `limit + 1` rows.

For infinite scroll, the client tracks `next_cursor` in memory. On a scroll threshold event, it sends a `FETCH_MORE` frame. The server streams back a `PAGE` frame with the next slice and an updated cursor. If the connection drops mid-scroll, reconnect logic can resume from the last known cursor without re-fetching from the top — a guarantee offset can never provide in a live stream.

bytes.topic: Filtering & Sorting — query param conventions, GraphQL args, protobuf fields
bytes.title: WebSockets Filtering — Query Params, Subscription Messages, and GraphQL Args
bytes.body:
The browser WebSocket API offers no way to set custom headers during the handshake, so query parameters on the upgrade URL become your primary mechanism for initial filter state: `new WebSocket('wss://api.example.com/events?topic=payments&minAmount=500&sort=desc')`. Server-side, parse these in the HTTP upgrade handler before the connection is promoted — reject invalid filters with a `400` before the socket is ever opened.

For runtime re-filtering without reconnecting, define a typed control message: `{ type: 'subscribe', filter: { topic, minAmount }, sort: { field, dir } }`. The server updates the per-connection filter state and drains any buffered messages that no longer match.

With GraphQL subscriptions, the same logic moves into resolver args: `subscription { paymentStream(minAmount: 500, sort: DESC) { id, amount } }`. The resolver receives `args` at subscription setup time — validated once, applied on every publish.

For Protobuf over WebSockets, encode your filter as a `SubscribeRequest` message with dedicated scalar fields and an `enum SortDir` rather than freeform strings, giving you schema enforcement and compact framing on high-frequency streams.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — consistent error format across protocols, problem+json
bytes.title: WebSockets Error Handling — Structured Errors Without HTTP's Problem+JSON
bytes.body: WebSockets lack HTTP status codes, so ad-hoc error strings leak into client logic fast. The fix: mirror RFC 9457 Problem+JSON inside every message payload. Define a typed envelope where every frame — success or failure — carries a `type` discriminant. On error, include `type: 'error'`, a `code` (machine-readable, namespaced like `ws://errors/rate-limited`), a human `detail`, and optional `extensions` for domain data. Close frames use WebSocket close codes (1008 for policy violation, 1011 for internal error), but these are coarse-grained — the structured payload carries the real diagnostic. On the server, catch all handler errors at the message-dispatch layer and serialize them into this envelope before writing to the socket. Never let unhandled rejections silently close the connection with code 1006 (abnormal closure), which gives clients zero context. Clients pattern-match on `msg.code` for retry logic, user messaging, and telemetry. This keeps your WebSocket protocol as self-documenting as a REST API.

bytes.topic: Authentication — OAuth2, JWT in headers, API keys, mTLS
bytes.title: WebSockets Authentication — JWT, API Keys, and mTLS at the Upgrade Boundary
bytes.body:
WebSocket authentication must happen at the HTTP upgrade handshake — once the connection is established, there's no in-band re-auth mechanism without a full reconnect. This constraint shapes every auth pattern.

Browsers can't set custom headers on WebSocket connections, so passing a JWT in an Authorization header is impossible from the browser. The two practical alternatives: encode the token as a Sec-WebSocket-Protocol subprotocol value (client sends `new WebSocket(url, ['jwt.eyJ...'])`), or use first-message auth where the server holds the socket in an unauthenticated state for a short TTL, then drops it if no valid token arrives.

Query string tokens (`?token=xxx`) are the worst option — they appear in access logs, Nginx/Cloudflare caches, and browser history. Treat them as plaintext.

For API keys in service-to-service scenarios, the Sec-WebSocket-Protocol trick works identically. For mTLS, client certificates are validated at TLS termination before the HTTP upgrade fires — zero token management, no replay risk, ideal for backend WebSocket services behind a sidecar or API gateway.

Always set a connection timeout on unauthenticated sockets. An open, unauthenticated WebSocket is a resource leak waiting to be exploited.

bytes.topic: Security — input validation, CSRF, injection, GraphQL introspection risks
bytes.title: WebSocket Security — CSRF, Input Validation, and Injection Risks You're Probably Missing
bytes.body:
WebSocket handshakes use HTTP, which means CSRF is real: browsers send cookies automatically during the upgrade request. Validate the `Origin` header server-side on every upgrade — reject anything not in your allowlist. This is your only CSRF defense since WebSockets ignore CORS preflight.

Once the connection is established, every `message` event is an untrusted input boundary. Clients control the payload entirely — parse and validate before acting. If you're framing messages as JSON-RPC or GraphQL over WebSocket, treat each `type` field as an injection vector. A client sending `{"type":"__schema"}` can trigger full GraphQL introspection if your resolver doesn't gate it behind auth checks at the subscription layer, not just at the HTTP endpoint.

For text payloads destined for SQL or shell commands, standard injection rules apply but are easier to miss since WebSocket handlers often lack the middleware chain that HTTP routes have. Apply parameterized queries and schema validation (e.g., Zod, Joi, or JSON Schema) explicitly in your message handler — don't assume upstream middleware ran.

bytes.topic: Testing — contract testing, mocking, integration tests per protocol
bytes.title: WebSockets Testing — Contract Tests, Mocks, and Integration Strategies
bytes.body: Testing WebSocket servers requires a three-layer approach. Contract tests lock down your message schema: define a JSON Schema (or Zod schema) for every event type — `chat.message`, `presence.update`, `error` — and validate both directions. Run these against a real server in CI so client and server never drift. For unit tests, mock the WebSocket at the `ws.send` / `ws.on` boundary using a lightweight EventEmitter shim; avoid mocking `net.Socket` or `uWebSockets` internals. For integration tests, spin up a real `ws.WebSocketServer` on a random port, connect a `WebSocket` client, and assert on received frames directly. Use `ws.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN` guards and promisify `close` events to avoid flaky teardowns. Test reconnection logic by forcibly destroying the underlying socket (`socket.destroy()`), not by calling `ws.close()`, which performs a clean handshake — that masks real reconnect bugs. Validate ping/pong heartbeat cycles independently: assert `ws.on('pong')` fires within your configured interval before the server terminates idle connections.

bytes.topic: Idempotency — safe vs idempotent methods, idempotency keys for mutations
bytes.title: WebSockets Idempotency — Surviving Reconnects Without Duplicate Mutations
bytes.body:
WebSocket connections are stateful and persistent, but delivery is not guaranteed. When a client sends a mutating frame — say, `place_order` — and the connection drops before an acknowledgment arrives, the client must decide: resend or not? Without idempotency, resending creates duplicates.

Unlike HTTP, WebSocket messages carry no method semantics. There's no built-in distinction between "safe" (read-only) and "idempotent" (repeatable) operations — your application protocol must define these. Query-style messages like `get_feed` are naturally idempotent; mutations like `submit_post` or `transfer_funds` are not.

The fix: attach a `messageId` (UUID v4) to every mutating frame. The server caches processed IDs in Redis or an in-memory TTL store. On receiving a message, check the cache first — if the ID exists, return the cached response without re-executing the mutation.

Set TTL to match your client retry window (typically 30–60 s). This gives you exactly-once semantics over an at-least-once transport, making your WebSocket API safe to replay across reconnects without corrupting state.

bytes.topic: Caching — HTTP cache headers, CDN, ETag and conditional requests
bytes.title: WebSockets and HTTP Caching — Why the Split-Hydration Pattern Wins
bytes.body:
WebSocket frames carry zero HTTP caching semantics. There is no Cache-Control, ETag, or CDN layer once the connection is upgraded — every frame is a raw binary or text payload delivered directly to your `onmessage` handler. This makes initial-state hydration the critical problem: clients reconnecting after a drop shouldn't re-stream megabytes the server already sent.

The pattern: serve your state snapshot over a cacheable HTTP endpoint with strong ETags. On connect (or reconnect), the client issues a conditional GET with `If-None-Match`. A 304 means the snapshot hasn't changed — skip the refetch, reuse the cached payload, then open the WebSocket immediately. A 200 means state diverged; update local cache before subscribing.

CDNs like Cloudflare and AWS CloudFront will proxy the WebSocket upgrade (`Connection: Upgrade`, `Upgrade: websocket` headers must pass through), but they cache zero frames. Configure HTTP cache aggressively on the snapshot route while leaving the WebSocket path untouched. This split — cached snapshot + live delta stream — slashes reconnect latency and eliminates thundering-herd refetches when thousands of clients simultaneously drop.

bytes.topic: Performance — payload size, compression, connection reuse, multiplexing
bytes.title: WebSockets Performance — Compression, Binary Frames, and Application-Layer Multiplexing
bytes.body:
WebSockets give you a persistent TCP connection, but performance isn't automatic. Three levers matter.

**Compression:** Enable `permessage-deflate` via `perMessageDeflate` in the `ws` library. It shares an LZ77 sliding window across frames, but the default `serverMaxWindowBits: 15` allocates 32 KB per connection. On high-concurrency servers, drop it to 10–12 — you trade ~15% compression ratio for 97% less memory per socket. Set `threshold: 128` to skip deflate on small control frames entirely.

**Payload format:** JSON costs you UTF-8 validation on every text frame. Switch to MessagePack or CBOR and send `Buffer` or `Uint8Array` — binary frames skip that validation step in the WebSocket framing layer and typically run 30–40% smaller on structured data.

**Multiplexing:** WebSockets have no native stream multiplexing — unlike HTTP/2, one connection is one ordered queue. For concurrent request/response patterns, embed a correlation `id` in every message envelope and dispatch responses by ID at the application layer. Never open multiple sockets per client to fake parallelism; you'll exhaust file descriptors before you exhaust CPU.

bytes.topic: Choosing a Protocol — REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC decision framework
bytes.title: WebSockets vs REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC — A Decision Framework for Real-Time Engineers
bytes.body:
WebSockets give you full-duplex, persistent TCP connections — but that's overkill for most API interactions. Here's how to decide.

Use WebSockets when your server needs to push unsolicited events to a browser client and the client talks back: live dashboards, multiplayer games, collaborative editors. The `ws.send()` / `ws.onmessage` loop is your primitive.

Use REST for stateless CRUD where the client drives every interaction. No persistent connection overhead, HTTP caching works, and your load balancer doesn't need sticky sessions.

Use GraphQL when clients need flexible data shapes plus occasional real-time updates. GraphQL Subscriptions tunnel over WebSockets via `graphql-ws`, so you're using WebSockets under the hood — just with a schema contract enforcing message shape.

Use gRPC for service-to-service streaming where you want HTTP/2 multiplexing, Protobuf-typed contracts, and bidirectional streaming without managing raw WebSocket frames. gRPC streams are HTTP/2 streams — you get built-in flow control and header compression that the WebSocket protocol leaves to you.

The deciding signal: unsolicited server push to a browser → WebSockets. Typed service mesh streaming → gRPC. Flexible queries + subscriptions → GraphQL over WebSockets. Stateless CRUD → REST.
